pi_items.source: task.json metadata field and confirmed in task description (line 17 of task.json)

Wireshark official documentation - all three tcp.analysis fields are valid and documented at https://www.wireshark.org/docs/wsug_html_chunked/ChAdvTCPAnalysis.html. Filters tested with tshark and syntax verified as valid.

File path confirmed in setup_task.sh line 13. PCAP file downloaded and verified from Wireshark sample captures repository at https://gitlab.com/wireshark/wireshark/-/wikis/uploads/__moin_import__/attachments/SampleCaptures/200722_tcp_anon.pcapng. File is 13KB, pcapng format, contains 35 packets of netcat TCP traffic.
File path confirmed in export_result.sh line 20 (OUTPUT_FILE variable definition)

Analyzed downloaded PCAP file with tshark using filter 'tcp.analysis.retransmission || tcp.analysis.fast_retransmission || tcp.analysis.spurious_retransmission'. The 200722_tcp_anon.pcapng file contains healthy TCP traffic with no retransmissions, fast retransmissions, or spurious retransmissions detected.

privileged_info_summary: The task creates a Wireshark display filter macro named 'tcp_bad_quality' with the expression 'tcp.analysis.retransmission || tcp.analysis.fast_retransmission || tcp.analysis.spurious_retransmission' to analyze TCP quality issues. The macro is applied to a real PCAP file (200722_tcp_anon.pcapng) downloaded from the official Wireshark sample captures repository, which contains 35 packets of netcat TCP traffic. When the filter is applied to this file, it yields 0 matching packets, as the captured traffic contains no retransmissions or TCP quality issues. The task requires the agent to create this macro in Wireshark, apply it to the PCAP file, and save the packet count to an output file.
